title: Closed-Loop Feedback Control System
description: A block diagram representing a classic closed-loop feedback control system, detailing the signal path: Input/Setpoint -> Comparator/Error -> Controller -> Process/Plant -> Output, along with a Feedback loop.
aliases: Closed-Loop Feedback Control System (Sem V - Control Systems)
theory:
A closed-loop control system, also known as a feedback control system, is a system that maintains a prescribed relationship between the output and the reference input by comparing them and using the difference (error) as a means of control. In contrast to open-loop systems, closed-loop systems automatically adjust their control inputs based on the measured real-time output behavior.

The system operates via several key functional blocks:
1. Comparator/Summing Point: Compares the reference input (setpoint R) with the feedback signal (B) to generate an error signal: E = R - B.
2. Controller: Processes the error signal (e.g., via proportional, integral, or derivative actions in a PID controller) to produce a control action/signal (U).
3. Plant/Process: The physical system being controlled (e.g., a motor, heater, or valve), which responds to the control signal and yields the output variable (C).
4. Feedback Element: A sensor or transducer (H) that measures the output variable (C) and converts it into a feedback signal (B) matching the dimensions of the input.

Feedback offers significant advantages: it reduces the system's sensitivity to parameter variations, suppresses external disturbances, improves steady-state tracking accuracy, and can stabilize an otherwise unstable plant.
keyPoints: Feedback is the defining feature, feeding output measurements back to the input for comparison.
Comparator generates the error signal: E(s) = R(s) - B(s).
Controller translates the error into a correction command to drive the process.
Sensor/Feedback path (H) measures output (C) and converts it to equivalent feedback units.
Reduces sensitivity to internal parameter variations and external environmental disturbances.
Can introduce stability issues (oscillations) if feedback gains are not tuned correctly.
examTip: The transfer function of a closed-loop system is T(s) = C(s) / R(s) = G(s) / (1 + G(s) * H(s)) for negative feedback. Remember that negative feedback decreases overall system gain but increases stability, bandwidth, and reduces distortion.
useCases: Thermostatically controlled heating and air conditioning (HVAC) systems.
Cruise control systems in automotive vehicles for maintaining constant speeds.
Phase-locked loops (PLL) in communications for frequency synchronization.
Robotic arm position and trajectory control systems.
Voltage regulators in power supplies to maintain stable output voltage under varying loads.
